IMPACT.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): This paper tackles a clearly important problem for the GEC community—dataset-agnostic, automatic annotation of error types—which directly enables fine-grained, per-error-type evaluation and analysis of systems. The rule-based, linguistically informed approach validated on CoNLL-2014 outputs and manual checks on FCE (with ≥95% judged ‘Good’/‘Acceptable’) promises a reproducible tool that avoids corpus-specific ML biases and is accompanied by released code. From a reproducibility and evaluation standpoint this will meaningfully change how researchers benchmark and analyze GEC systems, though it is an enabling infrastructure contribution rather than a transformative modeling advance. The method’s dependency on upstream automatic POS/parse quality is a practical limitation that will affect adoption in noisier settings. | B(4): This paper addresses an important and practical problem in GEC—reliable, dataset-independent annotation of error types—by providing a deterministic, linguistically-informed pipeline (alignment + rule-based classifier) that avoids corpus bias of ML taggers. The ability to produce detailed, per-error-type evaluation (demonstrated on CoNLL-2014) and high human-rated category quality (≥95% Good/Acceptable on FCE-test) makes the approach immediately useful for error analysis, system diagnostics, and benchmarking. While not a paradigm shift, the method and released code are likely to be widely adopted and to stimulate work on more interpretable, cross-corpus evaluation and targeted error modeling.

reviewer_a.justification: This paper tackles a clearly important problem for the GEC community—dataset-agnostic, automatic annotation of error types—which directly enables fine-grained, per-error-type evaluation and analysis of systems. The rule-based, linguistically informed approach validated on CoNLL-2014 outputs and manual checks on FCE (with ≥95% judged ‘Good’/‘Acceptable’) promises a reproducible tool that avoids corpus-specific ML biases and is accompanied by released code. From a reproducibility and evaluation standpoint this will meaningfully change how researchers benchmark and analyze GEC systems, though it is an enabling infrastructure contribution rather than a transformative modeling advance. The method’s dependency on upstream automatic POS/parse quality is a practical limitation that will affect adoption in noisier settings.

reviewer_b.justification: This paper addresses an important and practical problem in GEC—reliable, dataset-independent annotation of error types—by providing a deterministic, linguistically-informed pipeline (alignment + rule-based classifier) that avoids corpus bias of ML taggers. The ability to produce detailed, per-error-type evaluation (demonstrated on CoNLL-2014) and high human-rated category quality (≥95% Good/Acceptable on FCE-test) makes the approach immediately useful for error analysis, system diagnostics, and benchmarking. While not a paradigm shift, the method and released code are likely to be widely adopted and to stimulate work on more interpretable, cross-corpus evaluation and targeted error modeling.

ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper presents a notable, clearly differentiated contribution: a dataset-independent, deterministic rule-based pipeline that combines linguistically-enhanced alignment with rule-based error-type classification (using POS, lemmas, dependencies and Hunspell) as an alternative to prior supervised classifiers. This contrasts explicitly with prior maximum-entropy approaches that are trained on labeled corpora and therefore biased to their training data, so the method offers a meaningful new perspective for annotation/evaluation in GEC. The technical approach is a non-trivial engineering and linguistic integration rather than a simple reapplication of existing tools, and the high human-evaluation agreement reported supports its practical novelty. From a reproducible-systems viewpoint the idea is novel enough to inform experts while remaining incremental relative to entirely new algorithmic paradigms. | B(4): The paper presents a notable, dataset-independent perspective by combining a linguistically-enhanced alignment algorithm with a deterministic, rule-based classifier to assign GEC error types without labeled training data—this contrasts clearly with prior maximum-entropy, corpus-dependent classifiers (Swanson & Yamangil 2012; Xue & Hwa 2014). While many building blocks (POS/lemma/parses, Hunspell, enhanced alignment) are existing techniques, their non-trivial integration to enable automatic, training-free error-type annotation and per-error-type evaluation is a clear and useful novelty. The authors explicitly differentiate from ML baselines and prior edit-extraction work, so an expert would find the dataset-agnostic, rule-based framing informative even if it is not a paradigm-shifting invention.

This contrasts explicitly with prior maximum-entropy approaches that are trained on labeled corpora and therefore biased to their training data, so the method offers a meaningful new perspective for annotation/evaluation in GEC. The technical approach is a non-trivial engineering and linguistic integration rather than a simple reapplication of existing tools, and the high human-evaluation agreement reported supports its practical novelty. While many building blocks (POS/lemma/parses, Hunspell, enhanced alignment) are existing techniques, their non-trivial integration to enable automatic, training-free error-type annotation and per-error-type evaluation is a clear and useful novelty. SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper’s main claims are clearly stated and largely supported: a deterministic, dataset-independent rule-based annotation pipeline is described and evaluated on CoNLL-2014 outputs with human validation (FCE-test) showing ≥95% acceptable categories, and comparisons to reasonable baselines (max-ent approaches and prior alignment methods) are reported. Experimental methodology is generally sound—use of detection/correction distinctions, F0.5, and inter-annotator κ is appropriate—but some reproducibility gaps remain (no hyperparameter reporting for preprocessing/tools, and the approach depends on quality of automatic POS/lemma/dependency annotation). There are no evident mathematical derivations to check, and no major logical contradictions, so conclusions are justified though robustness analyses to upstream tagger errors and clearer implementation details would strengthen the claims. | B(4): The paper's main claims are clearly stated and largely supported: a linguistically-enhanced alignment plus deterministic rule-based classifier is evaluated on CoNLL-2014 system outputs and validated with a human evaluation (95% ‘‘Good/Acceptable’’), and comparisons to prior edit-extraction and ML baselines are provided. Experimental methodology is generally sound with appropriate baselines (Levenshtein and prior linguistically-enhanced aligners; ML classifiers) though statistical testing details are not reported in the summary. A technical limitation remains that the rule-based classifier depends on the quality of automatic POS/lemma/dependency annotations (acknowledged), which could affect robustness across datasets and languages — this caveat keeps the work from being flawless but does not invalidate the core results.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper’s main claims are clearly stated and largely supported: a deterministic, dataset-independent rule-based annotation pipeline is described and evaluated on CoNLL-2014 outputs with human validation (FCE-test) showing ≥95% acceptable categories, and comparisons to reasonable baselines (max-ent approaches and prior alignment methods) are reported. Experimental methodology is generally sound—use of detection/correction distinctions, F0.5, and inter-annotator κ is appropriate—but some reproducibility gaps remain (no hyperparameter reporting for preprocessing/tools, and the approach depends on quality of automatic POS/lemma/dependency annotation). There are no evident mathematical derivations to check, and no major logical contradictions, so conclusions are justified though robustness analyses to upstream tagger errors and clearer implementation details would strengthen the claims.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper's main claims are clearly stated and largely supported: a linguistically-enhanced alignment plus deterministic rule-based classifier is evaluated on CoNLL-2014 system outputs and validated with a human evaluation (95% ‘‘Good/Acceptable’’), and comparisons to prior edit-extraction and ML baselines are provided. Experimental methodology is generally sound with appropriate baselines (Levenshtein and prior linguistically-enhanced aligners; ML classifiers) though statistical testing details are not reported in the summary. A technical limitation remains that the rule-based classifier depends on the quality of automatic POS/lemma/dependency annotations (acknowledged), which could affect robustness across datasets and languages — this caveat keeps the work from being flawless but does not invalidate the core results.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: [Consensus avg=5] A(5): This paper addresses a core NLP task (GEC) with a dataset-independent, reproducible rule-based annotation and evaluation method and provides code, matching the scope and expectations of a top NLP venue. The methodology (linguistically-enhanced alignment + deterministic classifier) and evaluations (CoNLL-2014 analyses and human validation on FCE-test with high acceptability rates) use standards familiar to the community, and the authors acknowledge important limitations (dependency on automatic POS/parse quality). From a reproducible systems perspective it is well-positioned for the venue’s audience and has direct utility for GEC benchmarking and error analysis. | B(5): This work addresses a core NLP task (grammatical error correction) and contributes a practical, dataset-independent annotation and evaluation method that is directly useful to the GEC research community — both appropriate for a top-tier NLP venue. The two-step linguistically-enhanced alignment plus deterministic rule-based classifier aligns with community expectations for rigorous methods and evaluation (CoNLL-2014 analysis, F0.5 reporting, manual validation on FCE-test). Its focus on interpretable, corpus-agnostic error typing and public code increases reproducibility and impact, and the paper is positioned well relative to prior ML-based and alignment work cited in the summary.

reviewer_a.justification: This paper addresses a core NLP task (GEC) with a dataset-independent, reproducible rule-based annotation and evaluation method and provides code, matching the scope and expectations of a top NLP venue. The methodology (linguistically-enhanced alignment + deterministic classifier) and evaluations (CoNLL-2014 analyses and human validation on FCE-test with high acceptability rates) use standards familiar to the community, and the authors acknowledge important limitations (dependency on automatic POS/parse quality). From a reproducible systems perspective it is well-positioned for the venue’s audience and has direct utility for GEC benchmarking and error analysis.

reviewer_b.justification: This work addresses a core NLP task (grammatical error correction) and contributes a practical, dataset-independent annotation and evaluation method that is directly useful to the GEC research community — both appropriate for a top-tier NLP venue. The two-step linguistically-enhanced alignment plus deterministic rule-based classifier aligns with community expectations for rigorous methods and evaluation (CoNLL-2014 analysis, F0.5 reporting, manual validation on FCE-test). Its focus on interpretable, corpus-agnostic error typing and public code increases reproducibility and impact, and the paper is positioned well relative to prior ML-based and alignment work cited in the summary.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper compares against appropriate, relevant baselines for GEC error-type annotation and edit extraction (MaxEnt classifiers from Swanson & Yamangil 2012, Xue & Hwa 2014, Levenshtein and Felice et al. 2016) and evaluates on standard CoNLL-2014 system outputs with manual checks on FCE-test using accepted metrics (F0.5, detection/correction). Comparisons appear fair in that the same datasets and evaluation metrics are used and the paper acknowledges limitations of ML baselines and its dependence on automatic preprocessing. Missing details such as reported hyperparameters and deeper ablations on sensitivity to upstream tagger/parser errors are minor omissions that prevent a fully comprehensive reproducibility assessment. | B(4): The paper compares against appropriate prior systems (Swanson & Yamangil 2012; Xue & Hwa 2014) and contrasts different edit-extraction methods (Levenshtein, Felice et al.) using standard GEC data (CoNLL-2014) and F0.5-style metrics, which is appropriate. Comparisons appear fair (same shared-task data and common metrics) and the authors explicitly discuss strengths and biases of ML-based classifiers versus their dataset-agnostic rule-based approach. The paper clearly differentiates its linguistically-enhanced alignment plus deterministic classifier from learned methods and cites relevant competing work, though a few recent or alternative learned approaches could further strengthen coverage—hence not a full 5.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper compares against appropriate, relevant baselines for GEC error-type annotation and edit extraction (MaxEnt classifiers from Swanson & Yamangil 2012, Xue & Hwa 2014, Levenshtein and Felice et al. 2016) and evaluates on standard CoNLL-2014 system outputs with manual checks on FCE-test using accepted metrics (F0.5, detection/correction). Comparisons appear fair in that the same datasets and evaluation metrics are used and the paper acknowledges limitations of ML baselines and its dependence on automatic preprocessing. Missing details such as reported hyperparameters and deeper ablations on sensitivity to upstream tagger/parser errors are minor omissions that prevent a fully comprehensive reproducibility assessment.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper compares against appropriate prior systems (Swanson & Yamangil 2012; Xue & Hwa 2014) and contrasts different edit-extraction methods (Levenshtein, Felice et al.) using standard GEC data (CoNLL-2014) and F0.5-style metrics, which is appropriate. Comparisons appear fair (same shared-task data and common metrics) and the authors explicitly discuss strengths and biases of ML-based classifiers versus their dataset-agnostic rule-based approach. The paper clearly differentiates its linguistically-enhanced alignment plus deterministic classifier from learned methods and cites relevant competing work, though a few recent or alternative learned approaches could further strengthen coverage—hence not a full 5.

SUBSTANCE.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(3): The paper provides reasonable empirical support for its main claims: dataset-independent rule-based classification evaluated on CoNLL-2014 system outputs and a human evaluation on FCE-test showing ≥95% acceptable labels, and it compares to prior ML and alignment baselines. However, I see important gaps for reproducibility and robustness: no reported hyperparameters or sensitivity/ablation analyses (e.g., dependence on POS/dependency quality or alignment choices) and limited discussion of statistical tests beyond inter-annotator κfree. Code availability helps, but the lack of detailed experimental settings and failure-mode analyses weakens confidence in generality, so the evaluation is adequate but missing key experiments and analyses.  | B(4): The paper provides solid experimental support: dataset-independent, rule-based annotation is demonstrated on CoNLL-2014 system outputs and validated by a manual evaluation on FCE-test showing ≥95% ‘Good’/‘Acceptable’ category assignments, and it compares against prior ML classifiers and alignment methods. Related work coverage is described as adequate, and the authors acknowledge key limitations (dependence on automatic POS/lemma/parse quality and ML baselines’ corpus bias). Missing, however, are ablation or sensitivity analyses (e.g., impact of parser/tagger errors or individual rule contributions) and some reproducibility details (hyperparameters not reported), so while thorough overall the analysis has minor but important gaps.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper provides reasonable empirical support for its main claims: dataset-independent rule-based classification evaluated on CoNLL-2014 system outputs and a human evaluation on FCE-test showing ≥95% acceptable labels, and it compares to prior ML and alignment baselines. However, I see important gaps for reproducibility and robustness: no reported hyperparameters or sensitivity/ablation analyses (e.g., dependence on POS/dependency quality or alignment choices) and limited discussion of statistical tests beyond inter-annotator κfree. Code availability helps, but the lack of detailed experimental settings and failure-mode analyses weakens confidence in generality, so the evaluation is adequate but missing key experiments and analyses.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper provides solid experimental support: dataset-independent, rule-based annotation is demonstrated on CoNLL-2014 system outputs and validated by a manual evaluation on FCE-test showing ≥95% ‘Good’/‘Acceptable’ category assignments, and it compares against prior ML classifiers and alignment methods. Related work coverage is described as adequate, and the authors acknowledge key limitations (dependence on automatic POS/lemma/parse quality and ML baselines’ corpus bias). Missing, however, are ablation or sensitivity analyses (e.g., impact of parser/tagger errors or individual rule contributions) and some reproducibility details (hyperparameters not reported), so while thorough overall the analysis has minor but important gaps.

CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper is well organized (intro → method → experiments → conclusion) and states its main contributions early: a dataset-independent, rule-based annotation pipeline and per-error-type evaluation of GEC systems. Notation and methodological descriptions appear consistent and supported by experiments on CoNLL-2014 and manual evaluation on FCE-test; figures/tables and examples are reported as effective and the writing quality is good. Minor presentation issues remain: hyperparameters and some implementation details (e.g., sensitivity to automatic POS/parse quality) are not fully documented, which hinders immediate reproducibility. | B(4): The paper is well-organized and presents a clear logical flow from introduction through method (two-step alignment + deterministic rule-based classifier) to experiments and conclusion; the main contributions are stated early and the writing quality is reported as good. Notation and methodological descriptions appear consistent and the use of concrete examples (CoNLL-2014, FCE-test) and evaluation metrics (F0.5, detection vs. correction, κfree) aids comprehension. Figures/tables and examples are adequate for understanding the approach, though the omission of hyperparameter reporting and limited detail on some presentation elements suggests minor presentation gaps. Overall the paper is clear and readable with only small areas that could benefit from additional detail.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper is well organized (intro → method → experiments → conclusion) and states its main contributions early: a dataset-independent, rule-based annotation pipeline and per-error-type evaluation of GEC systems. Notation and methodological descriptions appear consistent and supported by experiments on CoNLL-2014 and manual evaluation on FCE-test; figures/tables and examples are reported as effective and the writing quality is good. Minor presentation issues remain: hyperparameters and some implementation details (e.g., sensitivity to automatic POS/parse quality) are not fully documented, which hinders immediate reproducibility.

Notation and methodological descriptions appear consistent and the use of concrete examples (CoNLL-2014, FCE-test) and evaluation metrics (F0.5, detection vs. correction, κfree) aids comprehension. Figures/tables and examples are adequate for understanding the approach, though the omission of hyperparameter reporting and limited detail on some presentation elements suggests minor presentation gaps. Overall the paper is clear and readable with only small areas that could benefit from additional detail.

main_claims: Introduces a dataset-independent, rule-based method to automatically annotate edits with grammatical error types for Grammatical Error Correction (GEC).

Combines a linguistically-enhanced alignment algorithm to extract edits with a deterministic rule-based classifier (using POS, lemmas, stems, dependencies and a dictionary) to assign error types without labeled training data.
Enables detailed, per-error-type evaluation of system outputs (demonstrated by annotating and analysing CoNLL-2014 system submissions).
Human evaluation found the predicted error categories were rated as “Good” or “Acceptable” in at least 95% of cases.
_summary.methodology: Two-step approach: (1) extract edits between original and corrected sentences using a linguistically-enhanced alignment algorithm; (2) classify edits with a deterministic rule-based classifier that uses automatic POS tags, lemmas, stems, dependency parses and a dictionary (Hunspell) to assign error types.
baselines: Maximum entropy classifier approaches (Swanson and Yamangil, 2012)
Xue and Hwa (2014) maximum entropy merge classifier
Previous edit extraction methods including Levenshtein-based (Swanson and Yamangil, 2012) and Felice et al. (2016) linguistically-enhanced alignment
metrics: κfree (for inter-annotator agreement in manual eval)
limitations: Machine-learning classifiers are biased towards their training corpora, motivating a dataset-agnostic approach (stated limitation of ML baselines).

The rule-based classifier requires automatic markup prerequisites for every token (POS tags, lemmatization, stemming, dependency parses) so its operation depends on the quality of automatic annotation.

reviewer_a.name: Reproducible Systems Engineer
reviewer_a.background: Senior ML engineer with 8+ years building and evaluating NLP systems; experience deploying GEC pipelines and conducting rigorous empirical comparisons.
traits: scrutinizes experimental design, demands clear baselines, ablation studies, and error analysis breakdowns
values reproducibility: expects implementation details, hyperparameters, datasets, and code or clear pseudocode to be provided
checks statistical rigor: looks for sufficient sample sizes, inter-annotator agreement, and appropriate significance testing
notices potential pipeline weaknesses (e.g., dependency on upstream taggers/parsers) and requests robustness analyses
reviewer_a.reviewing_style: Focuses on whether the methods and experiments are described and validated in a way that allows reproduction and fair comparison; requests additional analyses where needed.

reviewer_b.name: GEC Research Lead
reviewer_b.background: Academic researcher with a decade of publications in grammatical error correction and evaluation metrics; active in building annotated corpora and evaluation suites for learner language.
traits: assesses novelty and positioning relative to prior GEC annotation and evaluation work
values practical impact and interpretability: asks whether the approach yields useful, actionable error categories for researchers and educators
examines linguistic plausibility of rules and tag taxonomy, and whether categories align with established error tagsets
considers dataset-independence and scalability across languages/varieties and trade-offs of deterministic vs. learned approaches
reviewer_b.reviewing_style: Evaluates the paper's contribution to the field, how it advances error-type annotation and evaluation practice, and whether its claims about usefulness and generality are justified.
